topic: Area of Study 4: Music for Theatre
subject: music

items.q: What does the 'book' of a musical refer to?
items.correct: The script: the spoken dialogue and the dramatic structure into which the songs are integrated.
items.misconception: The book is the printed musical score that the orchestra plays from.
items.random_wrong: The book is the souvenir programme sold in the theatre.
items.why: Students hear 'book' and picture sheet music, but in theatre the term means the play-text and plot; that is why shows credit a separate 'book writer' alongside composer and lyricist.

items.q: Is a reprise simply an exact repeat of an earlier number?
items.correct: No - it usually returns with changed lyrics, key, tempo or mood to reflect how the characters' situation has moved on.
items.misconception: A reprise repeats the song identically to remind the audience of the tune.
items.random_wrong: A reprise is a brand-new song introduced in the second act.
items.why: The dramatic point of a reprise is contrast with its first appearance: the familiar melody heard in new circumstances shows character development or irony.

items.q: In an integrated musical, what is the relationship between songs and plot?
items.correct: Songs grow out of the drama, advancing the plot and revealing character rather than pausing the story.
items.misconception: Songs are decorative interruptions and the real story only happens in the dialogue.
items.random_wrong: Songs are chosen at random from the composer's back catalogue.
items.why: Since the mid-twentieth century the craft of musical theatre has centred on integration: a well-placed number does dramatic work that dialogue alone cannot.

items.q: In musical theatre, a 'reprise' is best described as what?
items.correct: A later, often altered, return of an earlier song
items.random_wrong: A dance break in the middle of a number
items.why: It's A later, often altered, return of an earlier song. “The opening orchestral medley” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: A musical that contains little or no spoken dialogue is described as what?
items.why: Correct answer: Sung-through. “A revue” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: What is the traditional function of an overture?
items.correct: To preview the show's main melodies before the curtain rises
items.why: It's To preview the show's main melodies before the curtain rises. “To accompany the interval” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: A vamp is used in theatre scores to do what?
items.correct: Loop a short accompaniment flexibly under dialogue until the song begins
items.random_wrong: Signal the end of the show
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Loop a short accompaniment flexibly under dialogue until the song begins. “Modulate to the relative minor” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.
